**Author Biographies**:

**Ziqi Kang** is a PhD student in bioinformatics and computational
biology at University of Helsinki, Finland. Her research is focusing on
profiling the tumor microenvironment.

**Angela Szabo** is a bioinformatician in the field of multiplexed
immunofluorescence image analysis at University of Helsinki.

**Teodora Farago** is a bioinformatician in the field of multiplexed
immunofluorescence image analysis at University of Helsinki.

**Fernando Perez-Villatoro** is a PhD student in Bioinformatics at

is a bioinformatician in the field of multiplexed
immunofluorescence image analysis at University of Helsinki.

**Fernando Perez-Villatoro** is a PhD student in Bioinformatics at
University of Helsinki. His research is focusing on the immune response
of the ovarian tumor microenvironment.

**Inga-Maria Launonen** is an MD Ph.D. student at University of
Helsinki. Her research interest is delineating therapy responses in the
tumor microenvironment.

**Ella Anttila** is a BMed student at University of Helsinki.

**Kevin Elias** is a gynecologic oncologist and surgeon-scientist at
Dana-Farber and Brigham and Women\'s Hospital.

**Julia Casado** is a former Postdoc at University of Helsinki. Her
research focused on the tumor microenvironment from highly multiplexed
images of tumor tissue.

**Peter K. Sorger** is a systems and cancer biologist and Otto Krayer
Professor of Systems Pharmacology in the Department of Systems Biology
at Harvard Medical School.

**Anniina Färkkilä** is an Assistant professor in translational
gynecologic oncology and a Specialist in gynecology at the University of
Helsinki.

**Abstract**

**Multiplexed imaging at single-cell resolution has been widely adapted
to decipher the role of the tumor microenvironment in cancer and other
complex diseases. Accurate cell-type phenotyping based on marker

gle-cell resolution has been widely adapted
to decipher the role of the tumor microenvironment in cancer and other
complex diseases. Accurate cell-type phenotyping based on marker
combinations is crucial but challenging because of (i) fluorescence
noise and batch effects, (ii) segmentation artifacts, (iii) laborious
annotation of the ground truth, and (iv) difficulty in reproducing
human-biased thresholding. Here, we present Tribus, an interactive
knowledge-based classifier designed for multiplexed image and proteomic
datasets that avoids hard-set thresholds and manual labeling. We
validated the efficiency and accuracy of Tribus by benchmarking with two
similar methods on four public datasets with manual labels. Tribus can
recover fine-grained cell types as accurately as human experts, while
allowing the discovery of phenotypically new subtypes. We selected two
large in-house whole-slide image datasets to show Tribus yields rapid
and accurate cell type calling in practical applications. Tribus is an
open-source package and comes with a user-friendly Napari plug-in
enabling integration with image analysis workflows for fast
visualization and interpretation for biological discovery from highly
multiplexed images.**

**Keywords:** Cell phenotyping, single-cell proteomics, Hyperplexed
imaging, tissue-based Cyclic Immunofluorescence (tCyCIF)

# **INTRODUCTION**

rom highly
multiplexed images.**

**Keywords:** Cell phenotyping, single-cell proteomics, Hyperplexed
imaging, tissue-based Cyclic Immunofluorescence (tCyCIF)

# **INTRODUCTION**

Multiplexed imaging techniques at single-cell resolution, such as cyclic
immunofluorescence (CyCIF) [(Lin et al.,
2018)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KROv5k), co-detection by
indexing (CODEX) [(Black et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JGGE22), and multiplexed ion
beam imaging by time of flight (MIBI-TOF) [(Keren et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QIwFvh), have developed
significant advantages for understanding tissue architecture. Such
techniques allow researchers to measure dozens of proteins at
single-cell resolution while retaining the spatial information of origin
in the tissue section. These experimental workflows generate datasets
that include multiple features and provide novel insights into the
diversity of cellular phenotypes and tissue behaviors [(Spitzer & Nolan,
2016)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8HFLes).

Cell-type annotation has become one of the most essential tasks and a
bottleneck in gaining full potential from multiplexed imaging
technologies. The main obstacles to cell-type phenotyping on multiplexed
images are the unexpected combinations of markers, signal noise, and
reproducibility limits caused by imperfect cell segmentation,

bstacles to cell-type phenotyping on multiplexed
images are the unexpected combinations of markers, signal noise, and
reproducibility limits caused by imperfect cell segmentation,
pre-processing artifacts, or tissue processing artifacts, such as
autofluorescence. It is difficult to apply popular scRNA-seq cell-type
callers [(Xie et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?QShDIL)
to multiplexed imaging datasets because they are not designed to
overcome segmentation noise, which does not exist in the scRNA-seq data.
The order-of-magnitude difference in the number of features is also an
important problem. Several markers can dominate the clustering process
in multiplexed imaging techniques, and thousands of genes have been
provided to scRNA-seq callers to overcome this problem.

Gating [(Staats et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7mgQgW) is a classical
cell-type annotation method that requires manual identification of cell
types based on the pattern of features visualized using histograms and
bivariate plots. However, with the increasing size of the marker panel,
manual gating becomes time-consuming and sensitive to batch effects
[(Verschoor et al., 2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?beDAnS),
which is no longer feasible. Unsupervised clustering tools such as
PhenoGraph [(Levine et al.,

atch effects
[(Verschoor et al., 2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?beDAnS),
which is no longer feasible. Unsupervised clustering tools such as
PhenoGraph [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fqmsl5), Leiden [(Traag et
al., 2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?U71hm0), and DBscan
[(Ester et al., 1996)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?L0oHCj) have
been applied to perform automatic data exploration [(Liu et al.,
2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?hnJI58). However, manual
verification is still required to assign meaningful cell types to the
obtained clusters. To perform a deeper profiling of cell types, it is
usually necessary to over-cluster and then merge the clusters, which is
both computationally and time-consuming. An alternative approach is to
use supervised or semi-supervised machine learning methods [(Abdelaal et
al., 2019)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?49sLMo), which were
developed to perform accurate auto cell-type classification. However,
such methods require pre-training on manually gated datasets according
to various experimental designs, thus requiring extra human effort and
lacking extendibility.

Here, we introduce the design of a novel cell-type caller named the
Tribus. Tribus performs reproducible automatic cell-type annotations
across variable high-plex imaging and proteomic datasets without manual

of a novel cell-type caller named the
Tribus. Tribus performs reproducible automatic cell-type annotations
across variable high-plex imaging and proteomic datasets without manual
thresholding. Tribus incorporates a widely used self-organizing map
(SOM) [(Kohonen, 1982)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Yl4HID)
unsupervised neural network model with a unique scoring function to
assign cell types according to prior biological knowledge. An integrated
Napari plugin allows for fast user interaction and curation. We
validated the accuracy of Tribus on four public multiplexed imaging and
non-spatial proteomic datasets generated by the CODEX and mass cytometry
methods. We show that Tribus outperforms two similar knowledge-based
cell-type identification approaches: ACDC [(Lee et al.,
2017)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?J4be42) and Astir [(Geuenich
et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?zlhfxw), and
demonstrate the utility of Tribus in the analysis of two large in-house
CyCIF datasets. Overall, Tribus represents a new user-friendly framework
for semi-automated cell-type calling for multiplexed imaging and
proteomics techniques.

# **Methods**

## **The structure of Tribus**

To run Tribus, a marker expression table and prior knowledge-based logic
table are required as inputs. The logic table can be defined as a data

**

## **The structure of Tribus**

To run Tribus, a marker expression table and prior knowledge-based logic
table are required as inputs. The logic table can be defined as a data
matrix $L\left( c_{i},m_{j} \right)$ containing values
$\lbrack - 1,0,1\rbrack$, where $c_{i}$ is the cell type $i_{th}$ and
$m_{j}$ is the marker $j_{th}$. If a marker is present in a specific
cell type, the value in the logic table is assigned a value of 1. If a
marker is supposed to be absent in a specific cell type, then the value
in the logic table should be assigned as -1. A score of 0 was assigned
for neutral or unknown markers. Each cell type had at least one positive
marker in the logic table (Supplementary Table 1-3).

Tribus consists of two main parts. First, Tribus uses unsupervised
learning to find clusters from the input data, and then assigns each
cluster to a certain cell type based on the logic table. The cell type
assignment is performed hierarchically, which means that Tribus first
assigns lower-level cell types and then assigns higher-level cell types
to create more precise categories.

## **Unsupervised learning in Tribus**

Tribus uses a self-organizing map (SOM) for clustering based on the
MiniSOM package [(Vettigli,
2013/2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ghMcaA). SOM can
represent an input space with high dimensions as a map space, which

ering based on the
MiniSOM package [(Vettigli,
2013/2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ghMcaA). SOM can
represent an input space with high dimensions as a map space, which
consists of components called \"nodes\". Quantization error (Q) was used
to evaluate the algorithm performance, which was calculated by
determining the average distance of the sample vectors to the cluster
centroids.

$Q = \ \frac{1}{m}{\|{x⃑}^{q} - {{w⃑}_{i}}^{q}\|}^{2}$

Quantization errors could only be compared under the same grid size
[(Pölzlbauer, n.d.)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7mS4PU). To
determine the optimal grid size, we used the approach of Vesanto
[(Vesanto & Alhoniemi,
2000)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KEe5Lj): $G = 5$, where $N$
is the input data size (i.e., number of cells). The parameters of the
SOM, including $\sigma$ (the spread of the neighborhood function) and
the learning rate, were optimized by minimizing the quantization error.
Hyperparameter tuning was implemented based on the package hyperopt
[(Bergstra et al., 2013)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?fTIX2g),
where the objective function was to minimize the quantization error, and
the search space for $\sigma$ and the learning rate ranged from 0.001 to
5.

## **Cell type assignment by scoring functions**

After SOM clustering, a node matrix $N\left( n_{k},v_{j} \right)$ is

for $\sigma$ and the learning rate ranged from 0.001 to
5.

## **Cell type assignment by scoring functions**

After SOM clustering, a node matrix $N\left( n_{k},v_{j} \right)$ is
generated, where $v_{j}$ is calculated using the median expression of
the marker $m_{j}$ in $n_{k}$. We designed a scoring function based on
the concept of squared error implemented in the QueryStarPlot function
of the FlowSOM package [(Van Gassen et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?G09YkV). This function
calculates the score $s_{i}$ of a certain cell type $i$ for each node
$k$.

$s_{i}\left( n_{k} \right) = \ \frac{1}{k}\left\lbrack 1 - \left( v_{ij} - \widehat{v_{ij}} \right)^{2^{'}} \right\rbrack$

where,

$\widehat{v_{ij}} = \{ P_{99}(v_{ij}),\ \ L(c_{i},\ m_{j}) = 1\ min(v_{ij}),\ \ L(c_{i},\ m_{j}) = - 1\ $

Instead of using the maximum value, the 99 percentile was chosen to make
Tribus more robust to outliers. Assigning node $n_{k}$ to cell type
$c_{i}$ using: $c_{i} = argmax_{s_{i}}f\left( n_{k},s_{i} \right)$

Note that if the maximum score of a cluster is smaller than a certain
threshold set by users, the cluster is labeled as the \"other\" cell
type. Similarly, if the difference between the maximum and second
maximum score is smaller than a certain threshold, the cluster will be
labeled as \"undefined\".

## **Napari plug-in**

imilarly, if the difference between the maximum and second
maximum score is smaller than a certain threshold, the cluster will be
labeled as \"undefined\".

## **Napari plug-in**

To efficiently assess and explore cell-type labeling results, we
developed a custom plugin integrated with the Napari [(Ahlers, Jannis et
al., 2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ZIFqeC) framework. This
allows the user to run Tribus over one sample at a time, display the
results simultaneously, or load previously saved data. The ZARR format
was incorporated to overcome the computational limitations associated
with large datasets.

The key functionalities of the Napari plugin include the following.

\(i\) Cell-type mask visualization: The plugin sorts and displays
different cell-type labels as separate layers using visually distinct
colors, allowing the user to overlay them with imaging data for quality
control. This function is also available in a stand-alone Jupyter
Notebook.

\(ii\) Probability score visualization: cell masks are represented as a
color gradient of the probability score assigned by the algorithm. This
allows the user to identify and check ambiguous cells and assess the
assigned "other" and "undefined" thresholds.

\(iii\) Marker intensity visualization: The median expression levels of
the desired markers are represented through gradient shading on the
segmentation mask.

defined" thresholds.

\(iii\) Marker intensity visualization: The median expression levels of
the desired markers are represented through gradient shading on the
segmentation mask. This allowed the user to visually assess the results
and identify potential biases.

## **Benchmarking Datasets**

We chose four public mass cytometry and multiplexed imaging datasets
with ground truth labels to test the performance of Tribus.

### **AML**

This dataset contains single-cell proteomic profiles of human bone
marrow from patients with acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and healthy adult
donors [(Levine et al.,
2015)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?a3cXqw). The AML dataset was
collected via mass cytometry with 32 markers targeting 14 manually gated
cell classes. The \"NotDebrisSinglets\" cell type was removed from the
analysis (N = 104184 cells). The cell typing logic table was designed
based on the cell type-marker table provided in ACDC research.

### **BMMC**

The BMMC dataset was obtained from bone marrow mononuclear cells (BMMCs)
[(Bendall et al., 2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NaqDnh) and
profiled using mass cytometry. The dataset contains 13 markers and 24
manually gated cell populations. According to the research,
Erythroblasts, megakaryocyte platelets, and myelocytes were merged as an
unknown population and removed from the analysis. All \"NotGated\" cells

tions. According to the research,
Erythroblasts, megakaryocyte platelets, and myelocytes were merged as an
unknown population and removed from the analysis. All \"NotGated\" cells
were also removed (N = 61725 cells). The cell typing logic table was
modified based on the cell type-marker table provided in ACDC research.

### **DCIS**

The ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) dataset was collected using
multiplexed ion beam imaging by time-of-flight (MIBI-TOF) with a 37-plex
antibody staining panel. DCIS consists of 69672 cells, 23 cell types
from tissues containing 79 clinically annotated surgical resections
[(Risom et al., 2022)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dJSFis),
including normal breast tissue (N = 9, reduction mammoplasty), primary
DCIS (N = 58), and IBC (N = 12). The cell-typing logic table for DCIS
was designed based on the panel provided in the published research.

### **HuBMAP**

HuBMAP is a published co-detection by indexing (CODEX) imaging dataset
collected from nine donors with a 54-plex panel targeting 25 cell types
[(Hickey et al., 2023)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?SrSjJ5). The
HuBMAP dataset includes eight regions (in the order of trajectory from
the stomach): duodenum, proximal jejunum, mid-jejunum, and ileum from
the small bowel, and ascending, transverse, descending, and sigmoid
regions of the large intestine (colon).

om
the stomach): duodenum, proximal jejunum, mid-jejunum, and ileum from
the small bowel, and ascending, transverse, descending, and sigmoid
regions of the large intestine (colon). In this study, we used manually
labeled donor 004 from the HuBMAP dataset (total N = 110635 for four
colon regions, total N = 137652 for four small bowel regions). Cell
typing logic tables were generated according to the panel provided in
published studies.

### **In-house Datasets**

To further validate the performance of Tribus, we applied Tribus to two
in-house high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) datasets generated by
tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence (tCyCIF). The first dataset
contains 21 homologous recombination-proficient (HRP) HGSC samples
stained with a 14-plex antibody panel (Supplementary Table 4). The
second dataset contains three images, including two tumor sections after
neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) and one treatment-naive biopsy. The NACT
dataset was stained using a 36-plex antibody panel (Supplementary Table
5).

Cell segmentation was performed using StarDist, and the mean
fluorescence intensities were quantified using in-house scripts. Hoechst
cycles were used to filter out the detached tissue areas and cells that
moved between cycles. Staining artifacts were removed by manual
cropping. The quantified data were further preprocessed with log

ilter out the detached tissue areas and cells that
moved between cycles. Staining artifacts were removed by manual
cropping. The quantified data were further preprocessed with log
transformation, z-score normalization, 99.9 percentile outlier removal,
and co-factor 5 arcsinh transformation [(Hickey et al.,
2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Tuc7St) were applied before
phenotype assignment. In the NACT dataset, 976,082 cells were annotated
using a cell phenotyping logic table based on panel design. For the HRP
dataset, approximately 10.5M cells were annotated using a constant logic
table.

## **Methods for comparison and evaluation metrics**

We compared the performance of Tribus with two similar prior
knowledge-based cell-type calling tools, ACDC [(Lee et al.,
2017)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?nMMQlc) and Astir [(Geuenich
et al., 2021)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1m7okT). ACDC allows
prior knowledge-based cell type calling by generating landmark points
based on a knowledge table and performing semi-supervised classification
on single cells. Astir is a deep recognition neural network that
performs fast inference of cell types based on prior knowledge of the
marker proteins.

We evaluated the overall clustering performance for all cell types by
comparing the Rand Index [(Rand,
1971)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XrD40k), Accuracy, Precision

ns.

We evaluated the overall clustering performance for all cell types by
comparing the Rand Index [(Rand,
1971)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XrD40k), Accuracy, Precision
score (weighted), and F1 score (weighted) [(Powers,
2020)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?419Lek). All the above
metrics were calculated using the functions provided in Scikit-learn
[(Pedregosa et al., 2011)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?4aKZIs).

The Rand Index was chosen to measure the similarity between two data
clusters and was defined as
$RI\, = \,(a + b)/\left( \left( \frac{N}{2} \right) \right)$. Accuracy
was defined as $Accuracy = (TN + TP)/(TN + FN + TP + FP)$. F1-score can
reflect the harmonic mean of precision (purity) and recall (yield),

$F_{i} = 2*\frac{\left( P_{ii} \right)*\left( R_{ii} \right)}{P_{ii} + R_{ii}},$

$P_{ij} = \frac{C_{ij}}{C_{ik}},R_{ij} = \frac{C_{ij}}{C_{kj}},$

where $C_{ij}$ is the number of events classified as population $i$
belonging to manually annotated population $j$.

# **RESULTS**

## **Overview of Tribus**

Tribus is a hierarchical framework for cell-type assignment in
multiplexed image datasets based on prior panel knowledge (Fig 1). The
prior knowledge is provided as a logic table, where markers are
specified as "positive", "negative" or "unknown" for each cell type.

When running the analysis, Tribus first performed unsupervised

s provided as a logic table, where markers are
specified as "positive", "negative" or "unknown" for each cell type.

When running the analysis, Tribus first performed unsupervised
clustering using a self-organizing map on the input dataset if the
number of cells in the given dataset was larger than the user-setting
threshold. Otherwise, Tribus directly calculated the scoring function
for each cell type at the single-cell level. The labeling process of
cell-type assignment using Tribus considers ambiguous results, which
means that Tribus does not force naming. If the scores of a cluster were
lower than a certain threshold for all cell types, it was marked as
'unknown'. Similarly, if the cell type scores are closer than a
threshold, the cluster is marked as \"undefined\" to avoid incorrect
assignments and facilitate the identification of new cell types.

## **Tribus recovers fine-grained cell types as accurately as human experts**

To test whether Tribus can recover canonical cellular populations, we
applied Tribus to the benchmark dataset DCIS. Tribus successfully
identified all populations highlighted in the study (Supplementary Fig
1A), and the median marker intensities of cellular populations were
similar to those of human-labeled populations (Fig 2A). One significant
difference was in the population of MACS (macrophages), which showed a

nsities of cellular populations were
similar to those of human-labeled populations (Fig 2A). One significant
difference was in the population of MACS (macrophages), which showed a
lower median intensity of CD14 in Tribus than in manual gating. This
might be because CD14 was not constrained to MACS identification in DCIS
research; CD14 was not constrained to be positive for MACS cells in the
logic table.

To compare how the cell type varied between manual labels and Tribus
labels, we mapped cell masks back to the original tiff images with a
nine-color overlay of cell identity-related markers. The cell masks
looked similar when we checked all major cell types (Fig 2B). We
validated the MACS population by visualizing the original images using
six related markers. We observed that more cells were assigned to MACS
in manual labels; however, the corresponding MACS markers were absent in
some cells (Fig 2B). With Tribus, we identified fewer MACS cases, but
with canonical marker combinations.

Comparing manual labels and Tribus labels, supervised UMAP visualization
of all cell types from DCIS datasets showed that Tribus could accurately
annotate the majority of cells (Fig 2C). Using the manual labels as
ground truth, Tribus achieved precision scores between 0.7 and 0.8,
average recalls around 0.6, and F1 scores between 0.6 and 0.7 across
most of the cell types (Supplementary Fig 1B).

d truth, Tribus achieved precision scores between 0.7 and 0.8,
average recalls around 0.6, and F1 scores between 0.6 and 0.7 across
most of the cell types (Supplementary Fig 1B).

## **Tribus can target ambiguous populations and discover phenotypically new subtypes**

One challenge for prior knowledge-based cell-type calling methods is to
discover ambiguous categories that are difficult to predefine in the
logic table. For example, there are groups of unknown cells with low
intensity among all the markers. The DCIS dataset is a good example for
exploring ambiguous populations because it includes "IMMUNEOTHER" and
"OTHER" cell types in manual gating. Among them, the \"OTHER\" cell type
shows low marker intensity in all markers while \"IMMUNEOTHER\" was only
highly expressed in CD44 and CD45.

We verified that Tribus can successfully target ambiguous populations by
setting the decision thresholds in the scoring function. We explored
different threshold settings and compared their effects on Tribus
performance (Supplementary Fig 2A-B). Based on the results, we chose the
undefined_threshold of 0.001 and other_threshold of 0.04 in the analysis
of DCIS. From the UMAP visualization, we could observe the same
"IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER" cell clusters with the same local structures
being kept (Fig 2C). The marker expression heatmap shows that

UMAP visualization, we could observe the same
"IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER" cell clusters with the same local structures
being kept (Fig 2C). The marker expression heatmap shows that
Tribus-targeted ambiguous cell types have the same marker expression
profile as manually gated cell types, such as "IMMUNEOTHER" and "OTHER"
(Fig 2D). We also discovered new clusters from the UMAP plots
highlighted with orange boxes (Fig 2C), including undefined tumors,
fibroblasts, and T-cells.

Novel marker expression combinations were observed using a median marker
expression heatmap (Fig 2D). We validated our observation by mapping
cell masks back to tiff images (Supplementary Fig 3 A-C). We identified
CD36+CD31+ fibroblasts and the HER2- luminal subtypes. Undefined T-cells
exhibit phenotypically higher marker expression than typical CD4T cells.
We suggest that Tribus be considered as a starting point to uncover
novel cell states via further unsupervised clustering.

## **Tribus outperformed other similar methods**

We benchmarked the performance of Tribus using two other approaches,
ACDC and Astir.

For AML and BMMC datasets, we used the knowledge tables provided by ACDC
and reformatted the logic tables/yaml files accordingly for
Tribus/Astir. No knowledge tables are available for the DCIS and HubMAP
datasets; thus, we generated knowledge tables/files for all methods

logic tables/yaml files accordingly for
Tribus/Astir. No knowledge tables are available for the DCIS and HubMAP
datasets; thus, we generated knowledge tables/files for all methods
based on the panel information. The ACDC analysis was performed with
parameters (n_neighbor = 10, thres = 0.5) in the example scripts. The
parameters for the Astir analysis were chosen (max_epochs = 1000,
learning_rate = 2e-3, initial_epochs = 3) based on the tutorial provided
in Colab.

Benchmarking experiments showed that Tribus outperformed the other
methods in terms of efficiency and accuracy (Fig 3A). In the AML and
BMMC datasets, Tribus reached a similar accuracy as ACDC, but with a
shorter running time of one order of magnitude (Fig 3B). Tribus
outperforms other methods across metrics on highly multiplexed imaging
datasets (Fig 3C), such as DCIS and HubMAP, and has comparable
performance on non-multiplexed AML and BMMC datasets. Tribus
successfully identified all cell types highlighted in the four public
datasets (Supplementary Fig 4 A-D).

We then explored how the structure of the logic table affected accuracy.
Using the AML dataset as an example, we applied Tribus analysis with (1)
a logic table with only one global level and (2) a logic table that
includes major cell types at the global level and sub-phenotypes (for
example, CD16- and CD16+ NK cells) at the second level (Supplementary

global level and (2) a logic table that
includes major cell types at the global level and sub-phenotypes (for
example, CD16- and CD16+ NK cells) at the second level (Supplementary
Table 1-3). We repeated the Tribus analysis five times, calculated the
average metrics, and visualized the results for each logic table. When
adjusting the logic table for the AML dataset, using a hierarchical
logic table can help improve accuracy and increase the F1 score by 0.1
(Fig 3D).

## **Tribus yields rapid and accurate cell type calling in large whole slide imaging datasets**

For the HRP dataset, we used a constant one-level logic table for all 21
samples to identify tumor, stromal, CD4 Tcell, CD8 Tcell, CD11c+
myeloids, and IBA1+myeloids (Supplementary Fig 5A). We applied Tribus to
the NACT dataset guided by a four-level logic table (Supplementary Fig
5B).

In the HRP dataset, Tribus accurately identified six cell types and the
cellular proportion showed high heterogeneity among patients (Fig 4A).
In the NACT dataset, Tribus successfully identified major cell
phenotypes and subtypes that we were able to characterize based on the
available marker panel (Fig 4B). The UMAP projection showed clear
cell-type populations in the HRP and NACT datasets (Fig 4C-D). We also
visualized the UMAP projection colored by sample origin in the HRP and

4B). The UMAP projection showed clear
cell-type populations in the HRP and NACT datasets (Fig 4C-D). We also
visualized the UMAP projection colored by sample origin in the HRP and
NACT datasets (Supplementary Fig 5C-D), and the results indicated a low
batch effect in the analysis.

We demonstrated that Tribus can successfully annotate dense tissue areas
and create accurate phenotype labels for further analysis. The
multiplexed tCyCIF image from sample 06 of the NACT dataset showed
nuclei and representative tumor and immune markers (Fig 4E). Tribus
could accurately identify the CD20+, CD8a+, and CD4+ cell populations
within a Tertiary Lymphoid Structure (TLS) despite the dense
organization of these sub-phenotypes (Fig 4F). Tribus also annotated the
proliferating subpopulation of tumor cells and tumor-infiltrating CD8+
cells from a dense area (Fig 4G).

# **DISCUSSION**

Cell-type calling is a critical step in high-dimensional image analyses.
The growing complexity and increasing number of panels in
high-dimensional data necessitates the development of reproducible and
more automated cell-type calling approaches. In this study, we developed
Tribus, a semi-supervised cell-type calling analysis framework for
multiplexed imaging datasets. Tribus has advantages in terms of
efficiency, accuracy, user-friendliness, and reproducibility.

-supervised cell-type calling analysis framework for
multiplexed imaging datasets. Tribus has advantages in terms of
efficiency, accuracy, user-friendliness, and reproducibility.

Tribus was generated as an automatic cell-type caller for
high-dimensional data by incorporating biological knowledge from the
panel design into the analysis. This was performed through carefully
designed scoring functions based on marker expression per grid generated
by unsupervised machine learning, to minimize bias. When the number of
cells included in the clusters was lower than the threshold set by the
user, Tribus skipped generating the clusters and calculated scores at
the single-cell level. This flexible scoring function calculation
strategy enabled the discovery of rare cell types. Tribus was integrated
with Napari, and a plugin was provided to import the cell-type
identification result in one click, which significantly increased the
simplicity of interactive quality control. Such integration enables
convenient operation for users who are unfamiliar with programming and
the interpretation of biological contexts. Tribus was specifically
crafted for high-plex imaging and proteomic technologies and seamlessly
operated across various imaging platforms.

However, Tribus is not without limitations. Tribus performance is
strongly related to the quality of the input dataset and prior knowledge

rated across various imaging platforms.

However, Tribus is not without limitations. Tribus performance is
strongly related to the quality of the input dataset and prior knowledge
of the expected cell types in the user-defined initial cell-type logic
table. To assign a uniform logic table, the samples should have even
staining patterns, both within and across slides. Uneven staining
patterns and antibodies with suboptimal signal-to-noise ratios can
significantly affect the results of Tribus. In sub-optimal cases, the
user can create a hierarchical logic table in such a way that major cell
phenotypes with clear marker signals are separated first on different
levels. This can result in more accurate labels if distinct areas of the
image are affected. Tribus cannot find cell types that do not exist in
the input logic table, but it can return undefined cell types for
further exploration with unsupervised clustering methods.

Moreover, cell-type annotation from bioimages presents inherent
challenges owing to imperfect cell segmentation and collateral
spillover. Expanding nuclei masks by a few pixels can enhance
cytoplasmic marker visibility, as it allows better capture of the signal
when cells express these markers. However, in dense tissue areas, this
might increase spillover, highlighting the importance of the
hierarchically structured logic table. Careful design of the table can

e markers. However, in dense tissue areas, this
might increase spillover, highlighting the importance of the
hierarchically structured logic table. Careful design of the table can
aid in cell separation, as only a subset of cells is considered at the
lower levels during phenotype assignment. Typically, mild spillover
affects only part of a cell, whereas a true signal yields a more uniform
expression pattern and a higher mean fluorescence intensity. Tribus was
designed to include both positive and negative components of the
expected marker expression. We could also aid correct labeling in the
case of a mild spillover using a less strict logic table, where markers
with false positive expressions are set as neutral.

Overall, we propose Tribus as a fast, accurate, and user-friendly
cell-type identification method that can be integrated into multiplexed
image-analysis frameworks.

# **Key points**

- A semi-supervised cell-type identification computational tool for
 > imaging data was developed, enabling the fast and accurate
 > phenotyping of single cells.

- The tool was integrated with the Napari plug-in to accomplish a
 > user-friendly assessment of the resulting quality before the
 > downstream analysis.

- Compared to other similar methods, our method performed the best on
 > all benchmark datasets.

- Tribus avoids hard thresholding and allows the capture of ambiguous

analysis.

- Compared to other similar methods, our method performed the best on
 > all benchmark datasets.

- Tribus avoids hard thresholding and allows the capture of ambiguous
 > cell populations, thus enabling novel biological discoveries in
 > downstream analysis.
